planting.note: Plant tubers in spring when soil temperature reaches 70°F; in zone 9 plant April–June. In zone 9 caladiums are perennial — leave tubers in the ground year-round in frost-free areas, but lift and store in mild zones where frost occurs. White Christmas is particularly striking in dappled shade under trees, where the bright white leaves illuminate dark areas. Reduce water and allow tubers to go dormant when foliage declines in fall; store at 60°F minimum if digging.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, acidic soil — caladiums require warmth above all else and tolerate deep shade better than most foliage plants; White Christmas is one of the most classic and enduring caladium cultivars, producing large, arrow-shaped leaves with dramatic nearly all-white blades set off by bright green veins and a narrow green margin — the effect of a mass planting under tree shade is startlingly bright and luminous, as the white leaves reflect available light and appear to glow in shade conditions where most plants would appear dull. Caladium is among the most effective plants for brightening dark, shaded areas of the landscape, and White Christmas is specifically sought for high-contrast shade planting because its white leaves provide the maximum illuminating contrast. The tropical foliage effect — large, bold, dramatic leaf patterns — also works extremely well in containers on shaded patios and porches. In zone 9 White Christmas performs as a reliable perennial, returning each summer from established tubers with increasing size and vigor each year.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Caladium Production in Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Caladiums for Gulf South Gardens
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Caladium Varieties and Care

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September. Summer Wave Blue is a trailing variety that cascades beautifully in hanging baskets, window boxes, and over the edges of raised beds. It blooms continuously without deadheading in shade conditions where most annual flowers fail. In zone 9 it performs through summer heat better than most shade annuals when given consistent moisture and afternoon shade protection from the most intense heat.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil; Summer Wave Blue Torenia (wishbone flower) is a trailing, shade-tolerant annual producing a continuous display of snapdragon-like flowers with deep violet-blue upper petals and a pale lavender lower lip with a bright yellow throat — one of the most vivid blue-flowered shade annuals available. The Summer Wave series from Suntory Flowers was specifically selected for superior heat tolerance and continuous flowering without deadheading in shaded conditions where standard impatiens may struggle in humid summer heat. The trailing habit (spreading 24–36 inches on 6-inch-tall stems) makes it exceptional for hanging baskets, window boxes, and shade containers where the cascading stems display the colorful flowers at eye level. Torenia thrives in the moist, warm, shaded conditions that characterize summer weather in the Gulf South — zone 9 gardens with humid summers provide near-ideal growing conditions. The wishbone-shaped stamens inside each flower give the plant its common name.
sources: Suntory Flowers — Summer Wave Torenia Introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Flowers for Shaded Areas
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade-Tolerant Annuals for Gulf South

planting.note: Plant tubers hollow-side up in spring after frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. In zone 9 Nonstop tuberous begonias can be left in the ground in frost-free conditions as tender perennials, returning each spring with increasing vigor. For container growing, use high-quality potting mix with excellent drainage — begonia tubers rot quickly in soggy soil. Remove spent flowers to maintain the display, though the Nonstop series has better self-cleaning ability than older tuberous begonia types.

directions: Plant in partial shade to shade in moist, well-drained, rich, slightly acidic soil or container mix; Nonstop Rose is a Benary-bred tuberous begonia series renowned for its continuous, season-long flowering without the summer dormancy period that interrupted flowering in traditional tuberous begonias, and 'Rose' refers to the full, camellia-like double flowers of a rich, warm rose-pink color — among the largest and most impressive of any shade flower available. The tuberous begonia form — with massive, fully double, ruffled flowers reminiscent of garden roses or camellias in size and form — creates a dramatic effect impossible to achieve with fibrous begonias, coleus, or impatiens. The Nonstop series combines this large, showy flower with vigorous, heat-tolerant performance through the entire warm season, creating reliable, low-maintenance shade container displays. In zone 9 the long warm season brings out extended performance from established tubers, and the tropical origin of Begonia means it thrives in the warm, humid conditions of Gulf Coast summers when protected from intense direct afternoon sun.
sources: Benary Seeds — Nonstop Begonia Series Introduction
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tuberous Begonias for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Shade-Tolerant Container Plants

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost danger has passed and soil is warm. In zone 9 start in January and transplant in March–April. Rosa Bianca eggplants reach eating quality when the skin shows the characteristic white-lavender blush and the fruits feel firm — overripe fruits turn yellow and develop bitterness. In zone 9 eggplant thrives in the heat and can be grown as a perennial, overwintering with light frost protection and returning to produce in spring.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in the warmest available position; Rosa Bianca is an Italian heirloom eggplant producing large, round to slightly oval fruits with a beautiful rosy-lavender and creamy-white skin that lightens to nearly white at eating maturity — the name means 'pink and white' in Italian and accurately describes the distinctive pastel bicolor appearance that makes Rosa Bianca the most visually elegant of heirloom eggplant varieties. The white to very pale cream flesh has the mildest, least bitter flavor of any eggplant variety — traditional eggplant bitterness is largely absent in Rosa Bianca, which has a delicate, sweet, slightly creamy taste that has made it the preferred eggplant of Italian chefs for generations. The flesh does not need salting before cooking as most other eggplant varieties require. The large, bulbous fruits (6–8 inches across) have a spongy, moist texture that absorbs olive oil and seasonings beautifully in roasted, grilled, and baked preparations — particularly in traditional Italian dishes like caponata, parmigiana, and grilled vegetable preparations.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Rosa Bianca Italian Heirloom Eggplant
Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Italian Eggplant Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Specialty Eggplant for Florida

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil reaches 60°F; in zone 9 plant March–May and again August–September. Plant in solid blocks of at least 4 rows for good wind pollination — single rows pollinate poorly. Isolate from field corn, popcorn, and other sweet corn varieties by at least 250 feet or stagger planting dates by 2 weeks to prevent off-type pollination. Honey and Cream is a standard su (normal sugary) type — harvest promptly at peak ripeness and use quickly, as sugar conversion to starch begins immediately after harvest.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Honey and Cream is a classic bicolor sweet corn producing attractive ears with alternating yellow and white kernels in the traditional American bicolor pattern — it was among the earliest widely available bicolor sweet corn varieties and remains popular for its straightforward, reliable, pleasant sweet corn flavor without the extreme sweetness of modern super-sweet types. Honey and Cream represents the standard sugary (su) bicolor corn flavor profile that defined American sweet corn for most of the 20th century — rich, substantial corn flavor with moderate sweetness and a tender, slightly milky kernel texture when eaten at peak ripeness. The reliable production, good ear appearance, and familiar flavor make it a trusted heirloom-style bicolor variety for home gardeners seeking a classic sweet corn experience. The ears are well-filled and typically 7–8 inches long — a proper size for fresh eating and corn-on-the-cob presentations.
sources: Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Sweet Corn Variety Trials
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sweet Corn Production Guide
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Sweet Corn for Home Gardens
